one_line_takeaway: A cryptographic framework for LLM watermarks that only pre-designated detectors can read -- built generically from multi-designated verifier signatures, provably distortion-free in the public-key setting, extendable to let the provider claim ownership, with generation overhead comparable to prior public watermarking.
key_finding: MDDW is feasible and can be built generically from any MDVS while provably achieving distortion-freeness in the true public-key setting (which the authors argue prior publicly-detectable watermarking [FGJ+23] fails to do, since a public key lets anyone detect the watermark). Experimentally, text-generation overhead for their DDW and MDDW schemes is nearly identical to the PDW baseline and close to twice the un-watermarked LLM's generation time (consistent with the ~1.5x theoretical cost of rejection sampling plus signature/hash computation). Detection is cheap (single-digit milliseconds) but MDDW detection is markedly slower than DDW/PDW because it needs more exponentiations, whereas DDW/PDW use a single bilinear-map computation; the single-detector DDW is therefore recommended when only one detector is needed. Theorem 1 establishes that supporting off-the-record-for-any-subset forces watermark length to grow linearly (Omega(n)) with the number of detectors.
core_contribution: The paper initiates the study of Multi-Designated Detector Watermarking (MDDW) for LLMs: a public-key-style watermarking primitive in which a model provider embeds a watermark that ONLY a specific set S of designated detectors can identify, while every other party (including third parties who later obtain the detectors' secret keys) cannot distinguish watermarked output from ordinary LLM output. The authors' own contributions are: (1) they define the MDDW primitive (algorithms Setup, WatKG, DetKG, WatMar, Detect) and formalize its security notions (completeness, consistency, soundness, distortion-freeness, robustness, and off-the-record for the designated set), plus two optional notions (off-the-record for any subset, and claimability); (2) a generic framework constructing MDDW from any LLM (used as a black box) and any Multi-Designated Verifier Signature (MDVS) scheme, embedding an MDVS signature via hashing + XOR randomization + rejection sampling on token blocks; (3) a new notion of claimability for MDVS plus a generic transformation turning any MDVS into a claimable MDVS (CMDVS) using a standard signature, a PRF, and a commitment, which in turn yields a claimable MDDW letting the provider provably assert ownership of LLM outputs; (4) a more efficient single-detector Designated Detector Watermarking (DDW) built from a designated-verifier signature (DVS) with watermarks ~1/4 the length of the MDVS-based instantiation; and (5) an experimental overhead evaluation. Note: the survey of Piet et al., Zhang et al., Qu et al., and Christ-Gunn near the end is cited related work, NOT this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Watermarking LLM output to distinguish machine-generated from human text, but with RESTRICTED detectability. Adversaries/goals addressed: (a) outsiders/third parties must be unable to tell whether text is watermarked (distortion-freeness, in the multi-query public-key setting) so watermarking does not leak or degrade output; (b) a forger without the provider's secret key must be unable to fabricate text that a designated detector accepts as the provider's output (soundness / unforgeability); (c) designated detectors must not be able to convincingly prove to any third party that text was watermarked, even if they leak their secret keys (off-the-record property) -- giving users deniability; (d) two honest detectors must never disagree on a text, even an adversarially crafted one (consistency); (e) the watermark must survive limited edits (a contiguous unmodified segment of length delta) but become undetectable under heavy modification (soft delta-robustness); and (f) optionally, the provider (and only the provider) can later claim ownership of a watermarked text without being framed by others (claimability: correctness, claim-unforgeability, non-frameability). The paper is explicit that the scheme, like prior undetectable-watermarking work, remains vulnerable to empirical removal attacks (emoji attack, translation/paraphrase attack).
limitations: Stated or evident in the paper's own text: (1) like all prior undetectable/distortion-free watermarks, the scheme is vulnerable to empirical removal attacks (emoji attack, translation attack); the authors cite [CGZ24] that no undetectable watermark can be completely unremovable. (2) Robustness is only a 'soft' delta-notion and relies on a min-entropy assumption; robustness is proven analytically ((2*lensig+2)*l-robust) but NOT tested experimentally. (3) The experimental evaluation covers ONLY computational overhead -- no measurement of generated-text quality, and quality parameters (words per token) were 'set empirically... rather than from rigorous theoretical analysis.' (4) MDDW detection cost scales poorly (more exponentiations) and off-the-record-for-any-subset forces Omega(n)-length watermarks. (5) All proofs are in the random oracle model. (6) Claimability is optional and, if made mandatory, several security games would need strengthening (left to future work).

datasets_used: No text datasets in the usual sense; evaluation uses prompts fed to three open LLMs to time generation/detection
quantitative_results: Text-generation time of DDW and MDDW is nearly identical to the PDW baseline and approximately 2x the un-watermarked LLM's generation time (dashed '2xLLM' reference line in Fig. 11a-c)
Rejection sampling gives ~1/2 acceptance probability per token, so expected sampling time is ~1.5x the original LLM per token (rest is signature/hash overhead)
Generation time scales roughly linearly with output length, reaching on the order of ~250 s at 400 tokens for BLOOMZ/OPT and higher (up to ~400 s axis) for Gemma (Fig. 11a-c)
Watermark detection time is on the order of single-digit milliseconds (0-10 ms axis, Fig. 11d); DDW detection is comparable to PDW and significantly faster than MDDW
At equal output length, MDDW embeds only ~1/4 as many watermarks as DDW; equivalently the DDW-from-DVS watermark is ~1/4 the length of the MDDW-from-[AYSZ14] watermark
Theorem 1: watermark size must be Omega(n) for n = |S| designated detectors under off-the-record-for-any-subset + soundness
Proven parameters: MDDW is l-complete, l-sound (or (lensig+1)l-sound under strong unforgeability), l-distortion-free, (2*lensig+2)*l-robust, and (lensig+1)l-claimable
Implementation: Python 3.8.10, elliptic-curve groups with 128-bit key size, Ubuntu 20.04, Intel i9-10900K @3.70GHz, NVIDIA RTX 3080, 64 GB RAM
baselines_compared: PDW: publicly detectable watermarking [FGJ+23] instantiated with BLS signatures [BLS04]
DDW: this paper's single-detector scheme (|S|=1) from a designated-verifier signature [LV05, SBWP03]
MDDW: this paper's multi-detector scheme from the MDVS of [AYSZ14]
